body_txt: A Trial Balloon Question is when someone asks a "what if" or hypothetical question to see how people might react to a real situation they’re actually thinking about or dealing with. It could range from the silly, like being a fan of a bad sports team; to the serious, like being a lesbian; or the fantastic, like being something other than human . It’s a way of testing the waters without revealing something they can't take back. By framing the issue as something speculative, they can hear people's honest opinions before they commit to a decision, and adjust their plans based on how people respond. Of course, the truth will eventually come out, if it's not immediately caught onto when they ask. Even as a hypothetical, bringing up the subject can be a lot more suspicious than never mentioning it at all. If the question is "Would you still love me?", and the answer is "Mostly", expect it to be followed with Have You Tried Not Being a Monster? . If the answer is "Of course I'd still love you", the actual reaction when learning the truth could still go either way — the person might have an unrealistic picture of their own prejudices, or find it easier to accept when it's someone they don't know. The only safe answer is "Meh", really, as it would mean they couldn't care less, and this really wouldn't change anything at all. Due to prejudice, this trope can be Truth in Television for some people, notably LGBT folks who field a hypothetical question about another LGBT person to gauge the safety of coming out to someone before doing so. If anything, using this trope can help you determine who your real friends and family are. After all, you hope that the people in your life will love you unconditionally. Compare I Have This Friend , Comic Role Play , Confirm Before Reveal , Rhetorical Question Blunder , Suspiciously Specific Denial . Not to be confused with the Armor-Piercing Question , where the one posing the questions is trying to force a realization in another (hence "armor piercing").
